answer: In 'Yeh na thi hamari qismat ke visaal-e-yaar hota' (Diwan-e-Ghalib, chapter 204), Ghalib says that meeting the beloved was simply never written in his fate (qismat). The opening couplet establishes the central claim: union (visaal) with the beloved was not destined, and had the poet lived longer, only more waiting would have been his lot—not meeting. The poem deepens this through layered irony: the beloved's promise of meeting was a lie the poet lived on, yet he concedes he would have died from joy had he truly believed it. The beloved's own fragile, inconstant nature already foretold the impossibility of a lasting bond. Meeting is thus framed not as a missed chance or a lover's failure, but as a cosmically foreclosed event—something fate itself withheld. The ghazal treats the beloved's inaccessibility not with bitterness but with a kind of resigned philosophical acceptance, asking what use even the most intense longing is when the very meeting it strains toward was never decreed to happen.
